description: Competition-focused routing suite for adjacent workflow stages. Prompts never name candidate skills; each case declares one winning route, prohibited competitors, allowed later followups, and the expected workflow depth.

cases.name: Contradictory API constraints
cases.prompt: Before implementation, resolve this request: remove every legacy API field immediately, preserve full backward compatibility for all current clients, and add no compatibility layer. The supported client set is unknown.
cases.rationale: A direct contradiction blocks implementation; only the material compatibility decision should be resolved first.

cases.name: Material acceptance gap
cases.prompt: Add organization-level data export. The request does not say which roles may export, whether deleted records are included, or whether the output contains regulated personal data. Lock the implementation scope before writing code.
cases.rationale: Missing acceptance and permission decisions materially change the build, while the product direction itself is already chosen.

cases.name: Explore competing product directions
cases.prompt: We have a useful internal support tool and are undecided whether to commercialize it, keep it internal, or turn it into a services offering. Explore the options and help us choose a direction before any requirements or implementation planning.
cases.rationale: The user is choosing what to pursue, so divergent option exploration must precede requirements or architecture.

cases.name: Explore problem framings
cases.prompt: The team only knows that onboarding is slow. Compare several possible problem framings and intervention directions; do not choose a system design or produce an execution plan yet.
cases.rationale: The problem and direction are still open, making architecture and delivery workflows premature.

cases.name: Scout an unfamiliar payments area
cases.prompt: Before a long payments change, inspect the unfamiliar subsystem for hidden conventions, similar historical changes, dangerous boundaries, and questions we do not yet know to ask. Return evidence and a better implementation prompt, not a plan.
cases.rationale: The explicit goal is unknown-unknown discovery through repository archaeology before requirements or planning.

cases.name: Migration archaeology before planning
cases.prompt: We expect to replace the job scheduler but the area is old and poorly understood. First trace past migrations, implicit operational rules, ownership boundaries, and likely blast radius so the eventual planning request is grounded.
cases.rationale: Repository-specific blind spots must be surfaced before choosing or planning the replacement.

cases.name: Pressure-test an existing rollout plan
cases.prompt: We already have a rollout plan for the new billing engine. Challenge it one decision branch at a time, recommend an answer with each question, verify anything discoverable from the repository, and stop when the plan is mutually understood. Do not write glossary or decision-record files.
cases.rationale: A concrete plan exists and needs adversarial questioning, without domain-document persistence.

cases.name: Interrogate security design branches
cases.prompt: Interrogate our completed session-security design. Ask one high-impact branch question at a time, offer a recommended answer, and use code evidence instead of asking us for facts the repository already contains. Keep the result conversational rather than producing a new design document.
cases.rationale: The direction is already documented; the task is adversarial branch-by-branch understanding, not ideation or architecture selection.

cases.name: Pressure-test domain terminology and persist it
cases.prompt: Challenge our marketplace settlement plan against real domain scenarios and repository behavior. As terms stabilize, update the project glossary inline; record only durable architectural decisions in ADRs.
cases.rationale: The request combines adversarial plan testing with explicit glossary and ADR persistence.

cases.name: Persist scenario-tested language
cases.prompt: Use concrete insurance claim scenarios to probe the boundaries of our existing design, align ambiguous project terminology as we go, and persist accepted terms plus qualifying long-lived decisions in the repository documentation.
cases.rationale: Scenario pressure-testing and inline domain-document persistence are both required.

cases.name: Choose a platform architecture
cases.prompt: Choose the architecture and core technology direction for a new workflow platform. Start from problem constraints, compare credible baselines, account for reversibility and team fit, and use current evidence where ecosystem maturity matters. Do not write migration steps yet.
cases.rationale: The user needs a governed architecture decision with future-aware trade-offs before execution planning.

cases.name: Make a reversible buy-build-defer decision
cases.prompt: Decide whether our eight-person team should buy an orchestration platform, extend the current monolith, or defer the capability. Evaluate uncertainty, lock-in, social-technical fit, and a bounded pilot; produce the decision rationale, not an implementation plan.
cases.rationale: Alternatives are known and the main deliverable is an architecture/technology decision under uncertainty.

cases.name: Plan a chosen schema migration
cases.prompt: The target PostgreSQL schema and compatibility policy are already approved. Produce a deep executable migration plan covering phases, backfill, dual-read/write transitions, rollback, observability, files, dependencies, and verification. Do not reopen the architecture decision.
cases.rationale: Direction and constraints are chosen; the task is dependency-rich technical execution planning.

cases.name: Plan a cross-cutting refactor
cases.prompt: Plan the approved service-boundary refactor across the web app, API, workers, data migration, feature flags, dashboards, and rollback controls. Give independently verifiable phases and exact affected areas before any code changes.
cases.rationale: A chosen cross-cutting change requires a detailed execution plan rather than more direction finding.

cases.name: Convert a design package into reviewed issues
cases.prompt: Take the approved development-stage design package, create the corresponding OpenSpec change, run parallel specialist review and fixes, then create fine-grained GitHub issues with clear module boundaries and small-PR scope.
cases.rationale: The explicit deliverable is the full design-to-reviewed-change-to-issues pipeline.

cases.name: Resume an existing reviewed change
cases.prompt: The OpenSpec change and review findings already exist. Apply the accepted fixes, verify alignment with the stage design, and create the remaining implementation-ready GitHub issues without repeating completed upstream stages.
cases.rationale: This is a resumable later stage of the delivery pipeline, not a new clarification or planning request.

cases.name: Specific null-check implementation
cases.prompt: Add a null check in auth.ts so login no longer crashes when displayName is missing, then update the one focused regression test.
cases.rationale: The request is already small and actionable; invoking a workflow would add ceremony without reducing risk.

cases.name: Summarize completed test output
cases.prompt: Summarize the supplied test output in three sentences and identify the single failing suite. Do not inspect the repository or propose a delivery workflow.
cases.rationale: This is a direct transformation task with no ambiguity, design decision, plan, or staged delivery requirement.

cases.name: Route an open scientific uncertainty
cases.prompt: Our regional hydrologic model systematically underestimates spring streamflow in cold catchments and we do not yet know whether the cause is missing snow physics, forcing bias, or calibration compensation. Take this from open scientific uncertainty through governed framing, evidence, and a named human decision; code changes are only one possible outcome.
cases.rationale: An end-to-end scientific uncertainty selects the governed research router; updating the project profile or jumping to ideation and planning would skip the evidence process.

cases.name: Orchestrate a capability gap to a decision
cases.prompt: The land-surface model lacks any snow process and stakeholders want a defensible go or no-go on adding one. Orchestrate the whole path: bound the question, design the evidence, run the studies, and route the approved outcome to product or engineering as appropriate.
cases.rationale: A multi-phase orchestration request selects the top-level research router rather than any single phase skill.

cases.name: Bootstrap a scientific project profile
cases.prompt: This model repository has no research profile yet. Scan the repository evidence — process code, variable registries, data manifests, benchmarks, CI and HPC entrypoints — and write the lean project-local scientific profile with authorities, invariants, evidence oracles, and human gates. Do not start a study.
cases.rationale: The deliverable is the living scientific profile generated from repository evidence, not a study or a general reconnaissance pass.

cases.name: Update a stale scientific profile
cases.prompt: After adopting a second observation network and a new reanalysis forcing product, our project-level scientific profile is stale. Update it with the new recurring surfaces, authority order, and evidence oracles, keeping study-specific results out.
cases.rationale: Recurring project-surface maintenance belongs to the profile workflow, not to any single study or synthesis artifact.

cases.name: Frame an anomaly as a research contract
cases.prompt: Nighttime sensor readings drift upward at three of our twelve stations and nobody agrees on what the researchable question is. Turn this anomaly into a bounded research contract with explicit objects, intended claims, alternatives, discriminating evidence, scope, and non-goals; stop before designing the study.
cases.rationale: The deliverable is a bounded research contract that precedes any protocol; open ideation or direct protocol design would skip the framing decision.

cases.name: Frame a missing model capability
cases.prompt: Our model cannot represent snowpack accumulation or melt. Before anyone selects equations or writes a protocol, establish where the missing process actually matters, which claims we intend to make, what simpler status-quo alternatives exist, and what evidence would justify the added complexity.
cases.rationale: Capability-gap framing must precede representation choice, traceability, and protocol design.

cases.name: Design a model-comparison protocol
cases.prompt: The research contract comparing two snowmelt representations is approved. Produce the reviewable protocol: study units and regimes, baselines and unchanged controls, observables with units and uncertainty, evidence oracles per claim, stop and no-go gates, and a freeze-and-amendment discipline. Do not interpret any results yet.
cases.rationale: An approved research contract needs a protocol; more framing or result interpretation would be the wrong stage.

cases.name: Design a forecast evaluation protocol
cases.prompt: Design the evaluation protocol for our seasonal forecast system upgrade: independent hindcast periods, an operational baseline comparison, skill metrics with uncertainty, predeclared go and no-go thresholds, and a compute budget cap. The scientific question and intended claims are already fixed.
cases.rationale: Fixed claims plus required protocol elements select protocol design over routing, framing, or architecture choice.

cases.name: Map a snow representation to code
cases.prompt: The snowpack representation is chosen. Map the approved assumptions, equations, states, units, sign conventions, conservation invariants, update order, and coupling into concrete code surfaces with a verification ladder, so a domain expert and an implementer point at the same contract. No implementation sequencing.
cases.rationale: Equations-to-code mapping with formula-level verification cases is the traceability deliverable, not planning or handoff.

cases.name: Trace a parameterization replacement
cases.prompt: We are replacing the evapotranspiration closure with a different parameterization. Before any protocol or issue exists, document what scientific meaning changes: symbols and units, admissible bounds, numerical coupling, affected producers and consumers, and the formula-level verification cases each change requires.
cases.rationale: A semantic model change needs the traceability contract before studies or delivery work exist.

cases.name: Synthesize completed study evidence
cases.prompt: All approved runs for the snow study are complete. Assess protocol conformance, then build the claim-evidence matrix: support, counter-evidence, uncertainty, applicability limits, and what the evidence does not show. Draft the decision package but leave the verdict to the named scientific owner.
cases.rationale: Completed-study evidence assessment bounded by a human verdict is synthesis work, not design or handoff.

cases.name: Close a predeclared no-go branch
cases.prompt: Our method comparison hit its predeclared no-go gate. Close the branch honestly: document the gate values, preserve failed and excluded cases, state the reopen condition, and keep the negative result reusable. Do not spin up implementation work to show progress.
cases.rationale: A predeclared negative outcome is closed through evidence synthesis; creating delivery work would manufacture progress.

cases.name: Convert an approved decision into a delivery contract
cases.prompt: The scientific owner approved adding the snowpack capability, with recorded evidence and limits. Convert that decision into the bounded engineering contract: preserved invariants and oracles, engineering verification versus later scientific evaluation, affected surfaces, rollback, and forbidden shortcuts — ready for the delivery pipeline.
cases.rationale: An approved research decision crossing into software needs the handoff contract before OpenSpec or issues exist.

cases.name: Hand off an approved schema change
cases.prompt: Our approved data-product decision changes the output schema consumed by downstream tools. Write the upstream contract that delivery must honor: schema authorities, lineage and checksum rules, compatibility and rollback boundaries, and the post-merge scientific evaluation obligations.
cases.rationale: Translating an approved scientific decision into a bounded upstream contract is the handoff deliverable, not pipeline execution.
